title: composefs end state "v1" goal
body:
Edit: 2026-03-17: A lot has changed here. As of recently a lot happening on this in composefs-rs! Check out https://github.com/composefs/composefs-rs/pull/224 in particular.

## 2027 proposal: There's a new `cfs` storage driver

Add a new `cfs` storage driver to container-libs which deferred basically all storage and mounting to `cfsctl` (part of https://github.com/composefs/composefs-rs), which we would talk to over varlink.

When fetching from a registry, the relationship between the two would be circular:

podman ➡️  container-libs ➡️  cfsctl ➡️  skopeo|podman (proxy), see https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/674

To me the containers/storage aspect is most important (the on disk integrity), hence that's been my primary focus. The rationale behind continuing to reuse containers/image I still feel boils down to that explained here https://github.com/bootc-dev/containers-image-proxy-rs/?tab=readme-ov-file#why

Also tangential but important: A goal I also have is for flatpak to transition to the same thing i.e. we have flatpak ➡️ cfsctl-over-varlink.

It's interesting of course because *some* use cases (e.g. just running an app container as a systemd unit) in theory could actually be deferred to a combination of systemd units + a custom `RootImageComposefs=` or so (a lot of implications there, but just noting).

Of course...it would all be *way* more elegant architecturally if we had a Rust version of the container-libs side but I'm not so worried about that.

## Original issue

<details>

We have a [list of composefs issues](https://github.com/containers/storage/labels/area%2Fcomposefs) but in this one I wanted to try to explicitly write down my goals/requirements for where we need to go with composefs and the c/storage and podman integration to be what I'd consider "done" (or realistically, "v1").

I was trying to lay things out a bit [over in this issue](https://github.com/containers/composefs/issues/294) on the composefs side, but it probably makes sense to track from this project in a more top-down fashion.

In a nutshell:

- Must have an efficient mechanism to verify the integrity of a complete image before launching a container (`podman run <image>`) from it; corruption includes e.g. changing the `config.json` for an image, its set of layers, and of course the layer content. It's OK to default to *lazy* verification for layer content. The podman `use_composefs=true` doesn't use composefs for metadata, though in some quick testing, we do seem to be verifying the digest of the config using the manifest, which is great. 
- At the same time, to state the obvious: Must have baseline OCI features expected today around signatures, mirroring, pulling and pushing. Especially, must support storing individual layers, and layers that aren't changed across an image update should not need to be re-pulled.
- Must also have `podman image fsck $image` which does upfront (non-lazy) verification, supporting re-fetch of a corrupted image can be re-pulled from the network. Related to above: efficient verification of individual layers, avoiding re-fetch of *all* layers for one corrupted file.
- Must be able to hard require composefs+fsverity when starting a container image with `podman run`, and there must be a clear documented way to attach a signature to that image (whether sigstore or another scheme), and that signature can be verified and result in an [an OCI bundle](https://github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/blob/v1.0.0/bundle.md) that must be fully fsverity enabled and have its trust chained from that signature verification.

Ideally: Maintain sync between booted host container image and app container images (https://github.com/containers/composefs/issues/332 cuts against this but we really should share what we can)...this may end up with bootc images having two fsverity computations (one plain, one excluding UKI).

---

Additional desires/nice-to-have:

- https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/133 is a big looming one...I could be convinced that for the near future it's ok to tie our broader work on composefs to zstd:chunked, but if we do that it also becomes a forcing function for https://github.com/containers/bootc/issues/20


Downstream: https://issues.redhat.com/browse/RHELBU-2799

</details>

comments.body: As of recently a lot happening on this in composefs-rs! Check out https://github.com/composefs/composefs-rs/pull/224 in particular.

> I am not convinced about adding a new storage driver because it ultimately needs overlay to work, so all we are changing is how a layer is retrieved/mounted in this case.

Right, there's `cfsctl oci mount` which sets up a currently read-only overlayfs. Is your concern that for cases that are not using `--read-only`, we'd still need something like what the overlay driver is doing to store a writable upper?

If so yeah I think that makes sense and probably the way I'd structure things here is thinking of the `cfs` driver as actually more like an AIS, i.e. we stack `overlay` on top of `cfs` or so.

> > 
> 
> Right, there's `cfsctl oci mount` which sets up a currently read-only overlayfs. Is your concern that for cases that are not using `--read-only`, we'd still need something like what the overlay driver is doing to store a writable upper?
> 
> If so yeah I think that makes sense and probably the way I'd structure things here is thinking of the `cfs` driver as actually more like an AIS, i.e. we stack `overlay` on top of `cfs` or so.

structuring it this way though, will result in more mounts that we actually need.  For a container using composefs we need only one overlay mount, but if we mount each layer separately we end up with multiple overlay mounts.

We could have a varlink API in cfsctl that did the fsopen("erofs") + fsopen("overlay") for the readonly parts and passed back the fd...(okay yes, queue the issue of varlink not supporting fd passing except with custom undocumented systemd extensions - I have some bits on that).

But yeah, this is a good example of an important detail, thanks for that!

So one option of course here is to just carry reimplementations where it's needed; mounting could be one of those.

> podman ➡️ container-libs ➡️ cfsctl ➡️ skopeo|podman (proxy), see [#674](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/674)

This would be a very major break of the current “copy” abstraction, which is driven top-down as a streaming copy between `ImageSource` and `ImageDestination` and allows `skopeo copy` in all its forms and _mostly_ centralized features. Now admittedly the “pull“ (registry → c/storage) code path has always had a lot of special cases, but this would go _so far_ beyond a “storage driver” (“graph drivers” as is the current `c/storage/drivers` API, ~“create/mount/unmount/import tar/export tar/delete” a single layer at a time), where c/storage owns all of the locking “on top” of graph drivers, and the c/storage API is generally one object at a time (“create this layer”, “store this data along with the layer”).

How does `skopeo copy docker-archive:/dev/stdin containers-storage:[theDriverUsingAProxy]…` (or, worse, an entirely custom `ImageSource` written in Go) work? Zstd:chunked ranged pulls? Interactive progress reporting when the stream consumer moves out of the top level? _If_ the driver API is one layer at a time, sharing the registry token so that we don’t have to obtain it for every layer again (https://github.com/containers/container-libs/pull/415 )?

Sure that’s “just a small matter of programming” but it looks like a very wide API that would need to be ~set in stone (freezing any further progress) unless both ends were in a single repo and a ~single RPM.

I basically just can’t imagine that; we might just as well throw everything away and rebuild around a single Podman→storage sqlite database, with transactions at exactly the right boundaries instead of the current “obtain multiple file locks for a single metadata query for a single layer” API.

If the goal is to move some parts to Rust[1], I think the primary engineering is in figuring out a _narrow_, hopefully long-term stable, API. This would go _far_ in the other direction.

---


[1] Is it??? To me, the language is so much not a relevant part - I mean, the ergonomics is _personally interesting_ but making this the fulcrum to pivot the whole design around seems rather unnecessary; we can always tediously handle language boundaries. I _imagine_ there might be some desired features (or maybe things we have learned from and want to avoid) that are much harder to change than a language.
- Do we want to build things around a single central authoritative database (e.g. for some reference counting)?
- Do we want to introduce transactional semantics where there aren’t any? (Just now we are running into an issue where deleting a container and deleting the containers’ RW layer is not transactional, and the layer can be left behind; and we know for a long time that pulls are not transactional, users can cause a layer to be deleted with one image while a pull process needs it to continue exist for another image)
- For the above, do we need a daemon, e.g. to simplify the “distributed system” design or to enforce timely cleanup if a client aborts without deleting its exclusive resource?
- … A _system-wide_ daemon, to allow cross-user-account data sharing, while retaining R/W permissions and preventing unprivileged users from DoSing root by holding a lock? (“logically bound images”)
- Do we want to somehow integrate non-tar-like objects? (“much better, incompatible, zstd:chunked” is my favorite; or “AI model file without the tar wrapper” I suppose)

I don’t know what we want in _any_ of these questions, but just seeing these questions written down makes me _extremely_ reluctant to commit to a cross-process/cross-language API of any complexity. “$something authoritatively owns and reference-counts blobs, and gives out file handles and paths”, fine I guess. “Here is an API for making ranged requests and progress reporting over arbitrary file formats”, how will we know that will support _any_ of the things we might want?

> > podman ➡️ container-libs ➡️ cfsctl ➡️ skopeo|podman (proxy), see [#674](https://github.com/containers/container-libs/issues/674)
> 
> This would be a very major break of the current “copy” abstraction, which is driven top-down as a streaming copy between `ImageSource` and `ImageDestination` and allows `skopeo copy` in all its forms and _mostly_ centralized features. Now admittedly the “pull“ (registry → c/storage) code path has always had a lot of special cases, but this would go _so far_ beyond a “storage driver” (“graph drivers” as is the current `c/storage/drivers` API, ~“create/mount/unmount/import tar/export tar/delete” a single layer at a time), where c/storage owns all of the locking “on top” of graph drivers, and the c/storage API is generally one object at a time (“create this layer”, “store this data along with the layer”).

I am also not convinced we should aim for such a complicated setup.  container-libs should be able to write directly to the store, either through a separate Go library, or maintaining the implementation here as well.

FWIW a brain dump of the way I think about the lower layers:

- Most importantly, do we need / can we do a big bang at all? Going by very small incremental steps _probably_ doesn’t get us where we want to go, but it’s clearly feasible, while setting out to do a large project risks, as is the usual case, being preempted by new priorities one month in.
- At the lowest layer: **Do we want to explicitly model cross-account / cross-store sharing?**
  - = the “logically-bound images” problem, where image layers are shipped in an OS image, vs. run-time application updates, vs. a new image with a different set of layers.
  - That would _drastically_ affect the security model: `root` must ensure integrity for everyone; how do quotas work? user accounts must tell `root` they depend on an image, vs. `root` must still have the ability to ignore that and break them; user accounts might hold locks but must not DoS `root`.
  - … and imply placing privilege boundaries / RPCs at specific places. So, affects any related API.
  - Or do we decide not to do that, and rely on opportunistic reflinks only?
- **Transactional semantics of a data store**
  - We have recently gained, and strongly want to preserve, the ability to create + delete layers without holding **EDIT store-wide** locks through all the filesystem operations.
  - That also implies post-transaction cleanup: If an operation aborts due to SIGKILL or power loss, _something_ must clean it up afterwards. Currently we have cooperative operation with locks to mark “still in use”, that would be a DoS in a cross-account scenario. Alternatively, a daemon which enforces rules?
  - Ideally, we’d want “whole-image” transactions: deleting an image + all now-orphaned layers; pulling an image with all its layers, where we never leave around unwanted dangling objects. 
  - … but not _that_ transactional: If a k8s node is starting and has 20 applications scheduled, and those applications share a base layer, ideally we would only pull the layer once, and have the other pulls block on the pending layer. So that would _somehow_ need to be surfaced in the APIs.
- Only _after all of the above is settled_ would I even start worrying about specific on-disk storage formats (raw overlay vs. composefs) or image formats (tar vs. zstd:chunked vs. future-chunked vs. raw artifacts)

I don’t know, maybe the above can be neatly hidden behind a narrow interface; that’s not at all obvious to me. Meanwhile, introducing _two layers_ of indirecting the access to a registry adds a lot of complexity and I don’t see how that simplifies the hard things above (cross-account safety, transactions while sharing pulls). It‘s hard enough for me to think about the low-level storage goals and implementation _without_ dragging image pull RPCs into it.
